1: 'In that day you will say: I praise you, O LORD; though you were angry with me, your anger has turned and you have comforted me' — Isaiah 12 is a brief thanksgiving psalm (6 verses) serving as the doxological conclusion to Isaiah 7-11: the great Immanuel-messianic section. 'I praise you, though you were angry with me' (odecha YHWH ki anafta bi, I thank you LORD for you were angry with me) — the thanksgiving is grounded in the honesty of having experienced divine anger. The 'though' (ki) is not adversative but causal: precisely because YHWH was angry, the worshiper thanks him — the anger was disciplinary, and the turning of anger (shav appecha, your anger has turned) and the comforting (vatenachameni, and you have comforted me) are the outcome.
2: 'Look — God is my salvation! I will trust and will not be afraid; for the LORD, the LORD himself, is my strength and my song; he has become my deliverance' — the third citation of Exodus 15:2 in Isaiah (also Isaiah 26:1; the first was implicit in Isaiah 5:13's context): 'YHWH, YHWH himself, is my strength and my song; he has become my salvation' (Yhwh Yhwh uzi vezimrat Yah vayhi li lishuah). The doubled divine name (YHWH, YHWH) and the exact phrase from the Song of the Sea identify this doxology as consciously echoing the Exodus celebration. The eschatological victory over Assyria/Babylon/the nations is narrated in the same vocabulary as the Exodus victory — the new salvation is narrated by the old salvation's language.
3: 'With joy you will draw water from the springs of salvation' — the water-and-salvation image: ushe'avtem mayim besasson mime'ainei hayeshuah (you will draw water with joy from the springs of salvation). The 'springs of salvation' (me'ainei hayeshuah) connect to the gentle Shiloah waters (Isaiah 8:6) rejected in favor of Assyrian alliance, and anticipate Isaiah 55:1 ('come, all you who are thirsty, come to the waters'). Jesus applies this image in John 4:10-14 (the spring of water welling up to eternal life) and John 7:37-38 ('rivers of living water will flow from within them'). The drawing of water with joy is a harvest-festival image — the Sukkot water-drawing ceremony (beit hashoeva) of the Second Temple period was the setting for John 7.
4: 'In that day you will also say: Give thanks to the LORD; call on his name; make his deeds known among the nations; proclaim that his name is lifted high above all' — the missionary dimension of the eschatological celebration: the personal thanksgiving (Isaiah 12:1-2) expands into universal proclamation. 'Make his deeds known among the nations' (hodi'u va'ammim alilotav, make known among the peoples his deeds) — the same mission as Psalms 105:1 and 1 Chronicles 16:8 (David bringing the ark to Jerusalem). The 'name exalted above all' (nishgav shemo, his name is lifted high) becomes the content of the proclamation: YHWH's name, which the Assyrian king thought equal to national deities (Isaiah 10:10-11), is the name exalted above all nations.
5: 'Sing praises to the LORD, for he has done glorious things; make this known throughout all the earth!' — the call to praise (zameru YHWH, sing praises to YHWH) because 'he has done glorious things' (ki ge'ut asah, for he has done majesty/glory). The universal proclamation: 'make this known throughout all the earth!' (muada't zot bechol-ha'arets, let this be known in all the earth). The 'glorious things' are the specific acts of the messianic age described in Isaiah 9-11 (the great light, the Davidic child, the seven-fold Spirit, the peaceable kingdom, the second exodus). The earth-wide proclamation anticipates the global mission of the NT: 'Go and make disciples of all nations' (Matthew 28:19) is grounded in the eschatological proclamation of YHWH's saving acts.
6: 'Cry aloud and shout for joy, you who dwell in Zion, for great is the Holy One of Israel in your midst!' — the closing acclamation: tsahali varonni yoshevet Tsiyon (shout aloud and sing for joy, O inhabitant of Zion). The two verbs of joyful sound (tsahal, ringing cry; ranan, sing/shout) frame the final declaration: 'great is the Holy One of Israel in your midst' (ki gadol beqirbekh qedosh Yisrael). The 'Holy One of Israel' (Isaiah's signature epithet for YHWH, 26 times in Isaiah) is specifically present 'in your midst' (beqirbekh, in your inner parts/among you) — the divine indwelling that Zion was promised and that the messianic age brings to completeness. The short Isaiah 12 closes the first major Isaianic section (Isaiah 1-12) on this note of eschatological joy and divine presence.
